category_summary:
**Gary Marcus** dominated discourse with a [viral thread](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-c84bedf71245) (2,300+ likes) arguing **Claude Code**'s leaked 3,167-line symbolic kernel proves it is neurosymbolic AI, not a pure LLM — vindicating hybrid architecture advocates and challenging scaling-only narratives.

- **OpenAI** [disclosed an **Axios** supply chain security vulnerability](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-d762bf809d46) requiring mandatory macOS app updates (2.4M views). **Sam Altman** [acknowledged a "tough day"](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-6de30b4c81cb) amid apparent crisis, while Marcus [escalated direct criticism](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-b337d43a45cf) of Altman's moral claims around surveillance, creator compensation, and liability avoidance.
- **David Ha** (Sakana AI) [shared striking research](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-6915ade59040) on a "**Neural Computer**" using video generation architectures to simulate an entire OS interface — rendering text and controlling cursors without traditional computing, from **Schmidhuber**'s lab.
- **Tesla FSD** [began rolling out](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-d030bb2978a4) to customers in the **Netherlands**, announced by Autopilot head **Ashok Elluswamy**, marking a significant European expansion milestone.
- **Nathan Lambert** [warned that funding structures](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-015ffabbda4c) for frontier open models will collapse within two years as costs rise. **Ethan Mollick** [offered cultural commentary](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-e115d9cce4b0) on AI commoditizing substance in writing, forcing renewed emphasis on style. **Harrison Chase** (**LangChain**) [drew massive engagement](/?date=2026-04-12&category=social#item-b31f2cfeb6b6) analyzing platform lock-in dynamics around memory and context in agent architectures.

items.title:
Claude Code is not AGI, but it is the single biggest advance in AI since the LLM.

But the thing is,...
items.content:
Claude Code is not AGI, but it is the single biggest advance in AI since the LLM.

But the thing is, Claude Code is NOT a pure LLM. And it’s not pure deep learning. Not even close. 

And that changes everything. 

The source code leak proves it. Tucked away at its center is a 3,167 line kernel called print.ts.

print.ts is a pattern matching. And pattern matching is supposed to be the *strength* of LLMs. 

But Anthropic figured out that if you really need to get your patterns right, you can’t trust a pure LLM. They are too probabilistic. And too erratic.

Instead, the way Anthropic built that kernel is straight out of classical symbolic AI.  For example, it is in large part a big IF-THEN conditional, with 486 branch points and 12 levels of nesting — all inside a deterministic, symbolic loop that the real godfathers of AI, people like John McCarthy and Marvin Minsky and Herb Simon, would have instantly recognized.*

Putting things differently, Anthropic, when push came to shove, went exactly where I long said the field needed to go (and where @geoffreyhinton said we didn’t need to go): to Neurosymbolic AI. 

That’s right, the biggest advance since the LLM was neurosymbolic.  AlphaFold, AlphaEvolve, AlphaProof, and AlphaGeometry are all neurosymbolic, too; so is Code Interpreter; when you are calling code, you are asking symbolic AI do an important part of the work.

Claude Code isn’t better because of scaling. 

It’s better because Anthropic accepted the importance of using classical AI techniques alongside neural networks — precisely marriage I have long advocated. 

It’s *massive* vindication for me (go see my 2019 debate with Bengio for context, or to my 2001 book, The Algebraic Mind), but it still ain’t perfect, or even close.

What we really need to do to get trustworthy AI rather than the current unpredictable “jagged” mess, is to go in the knowledge-, reasoning-, and world-model driven direction I laid out in 2020, in an article called the Next Decade in AI, in which neurosymbolic AI is just the *starting point* in a longer journey.*

Read that article if you want to know what else we need to do next. 

The first part has already come to pass. In time, other three will, too.

Meanwhile, the implications for the allocation of capital are pretty massive: smartly adding in bits of symbolic AI can do a lot more than scaling alone, and even Anthropic as now discovered (though they won’t say) scaling is no longer the essence of innovation. 

The paradigm has changed.

—
*Claude Code is plainly neurosymbolic but the code part is a mess; as Ernie Davis and I argued in Rebooting AI in 2019, we also need major advances in software engineering. But that’s a story for another day.

items.title: A "Neural Computer" is built by adapting video generation architectures to train a World Model of an...
items.content:
A "Neural Computer" is built by adapting video generation architectures to train a World Model of an actual computer that can directly simulate a computer interface.

Instead of interacting with a real operating system, these models can take in user actions like keystrokes and mouse clicks alongside previous screen pixels to predict and generate the next video frames.

Trained solely on recorded input and output traces, it successfully learned to render readable text and control a cursor, proving that a neural network can run as its own visual computing environment without a traditional operating system.

https://t.co/roTpqsdrEE

Cool work by @MingchenZhuge @SchmidhuberAI et al.!
items.author: @hardmaru
items.summary: As first covered in [Research](/?date=2026-04-10&category=research#item-267d00239de4) on April 10, David Ha shares research on a 'Neural Computer' that uses video generation architectures to simulate an entire computer interface - rendering text, controlling cursors - without a traditional OS, trained solely on recorded input/output traces. By Mingchen Zhuge and Schmidhuber et al.
items.reasoning: Highly novel research concept from a credible researcher (David Ha, formerly at Google Brain/Stability). Strong engagement (57K views, 705 likes). A neural network that can simulate a computer environment is a conceptually significant advance in world models.

items.title: We recently identified a security issue involving the third-party developer library Axios that was p...
items.content:
We recently identified a security issue involving the third-party developer library Axios that was part of a broader industry incident. We found no evidence that OpenAI user data was accessed, that our systems were compromised, or that our software was altered.

Out of an abundance of caution we are taking steps to protect the process that certifies our macOS applications are legitimate OpenAI apps.

We are updating our security certifications, which will require all macOS users to update their OpenAI apps to the latest versions. This helps prevent any risk—however unlikely—of someone attempting to distribute a fake app that appears to be from OpenAI. You can update safely through an in-app update or at the official links below. 🧵

Out of caution, they're updating macOS app security certifications, requiring all users to update to prevent potential fake app distribution.
items.reasoning: Major security disclosure from OpenAI with 2.4M views and 5.5K likes. The Axios supply chain vulnerability is an industry-wide incident. The mandatory macOS app update requirement and fake app prevention measures indicate significant concern. This is a notable cybersecurity event affecting millions of users.

And yes, I hate consortia too.

items.title:
Someone built Andrej Karpathy's dream tool 48 hours after he asked for it. 

Graphify is an open-sou...
items.content:
Someone built Andrej Karpathy's dream tool 48 hours after he asked for it. 

Graphify is an open-source tool for Claude Code. 

Point it at any folder and one command builds a knowledge graph.

It reads your code, docs, PDFs, and images. 

No vector database. No config files.

What comes out the other side:

> Navigable graph of every concept
> Obsidian vault with backlinks
> Wiki starting from index.md
> Plain English Q&A over everything 

It uses two passes. 
1. First, it parses code structure without an LLM. 
2. Second, Claude subagents extract concepts from docs and images in parallel. 

Results merge into a single queryable graph.

Every connection is tagged as extracted, inferred, or ambiguous. 

You always know what was found vs guessed.

The efficiency gain is 71.5x fewer tokens per query compared to reading raw files. 

Subsequent queries read the compact graph, not your entire codebase.

Open source.

items.title:
this is a good point around taking advantage of model/api provider features

i agree that prompt cac...
items.content:
this is a good point around taking advantage of model/api provider features

i agree that prompt caching is great! we make sure to use it in deepagents! but that alone doesnt lock in - you can switch pretty easily, just a bit more costly

things like encrypted or serverside compaction make it harder to do so. blackbox long term memory makes it impossible

i think there is a line between optimizing a harness for a model/llm provider, and using the harness to create lock in for a model provider

i think model providers are inherently incentivized to create lock in, and i think managed claude agents is fantastic example of that coming true

i agree that prompt caching is great! we make sure to use it in deepagents! but that alone doesnt lock in - you can switch pretty easily, just a bit more costly

things like encrypted or serverside compaction make it harder to do so. blackbox long term memory makes it impossible

i think there is a line between optimizing a harness for a model/llm provider, and using the harness to create lock in for a model provider

i think model providers are inherently incentivized to create lock in, and i think managed claude agents is fantastic example of that coming true

items.title: “If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data, xAI providing the i...
items.content:
“If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data, xAI providing the intelligence, SpaceX providing the global connectivity — the result isn't three companies. It's one closed-loop ecosystem that no single-sector competitor can replicate.”

My read?

SpaceX is a few things.

1. A way to get things to space.
2. Datacenters on earth and in space. Infrastructure for building and running AIs).
3. Packet distribution from those datasets to humans and machines. (Starlink).
4. Infrastructure on moon and Mars (eventually).
5. AI to run and orchestrate robots (the brains of Optimus and some of Robotaxi network, both via Grok).

Tesla is a few other things.

1. Robot manufacturing.
2. AI for enabling robots to move around the world and, coming soon, manipulate it. (Runs the body of Optimus and runs most of Robotaxi network)
3. Infrastructure to run the robots (charge, clean, and maintain them).

Each of these two groups will be intertwined.

Especially in Optimus.

This is why I am not selling my Tesla stock. I see Optimus as part of the Robotaxi network as the most important new product introduction since the Apple II and will improve the lives of many more people than that did. I am investing in that.

When Optimus gets added to the Robotaxi network it will bring “everything as a service” to all of us which dramatically changes how we will buy everything.
 
This patent analysis shows that I need to sell something else and invest in SpaceX. Because when we talk to Robotaxi or Optimus or any other Tesla robot we will be talking to Grok. Made by SpaceX.

This morning I had breakfast with a guy who worked for Amazon robotics for a decade.

He couldn’t find holes to poke and didn’t disagree when I said I don’t see a competitor in the Western World for either.

This is why the SpaceX IPO will be the biggest ever.

Even though xAI is losing billions.

Or is it?

Instagram, on the day it was sold, was a money loser too. Now it’s worth 100 times more. Maybe a lot more.

AI is about to make X worth a lot more.

But, hey, not investment advice.

Do your own homework and come to your own conclusions.

items.title: A lot of our education on writing well focuses on logic, clarity, and argument. AI will force us to ...
items.content: A lot of our education on writing well focuses on logic, clarity, and argument. AI will force us to think more about style. The boredom that comes from everything on the internet reading Claude-y now, no matter how good the substance is, should make us appreciate (and make us want to develop) style.
items.author: @emollick.bsky.social
items.summary: Mollick argues AI will force us to value writing style more, since substance is becoming commoditized. Notes the boredom of everything on the internet reading 'Claude-y' and calls for developing individual style.
items.reasoning: Highly insightful post from one of AI's most respected commentators. Strong engagement (87 likes). Identifies an important cultural shift: AI commoditizing competent writing will elevate the value of distinctive style. The 'Claude-y' framing is memorable and captures a real phenomenon.

items.title: In the end, working with LLMs to build something AI-based is not so much different from what it was ...
items.content:
In the end, working with LLMs to build something AI-based is not so much different from what it was three years ago with regular neural networks: You still get some ~80% performance very fast and then you are stuck for a long time at getting to 98% by adding more and more examples (that is instructions) to fix the remaining 20% of issues.

One difference, though, is quite significant: with neural network training, you had a good chance to get to 98% if you were persistent and accurate enough in data labeling and your management was patient enough.

With LLMs, on the other hand, you pray Anthropic/OpenAI/Google will "drop" a better model which gives more fucks about your carefully crafted last-mile instructions.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov observes that working with LLMs to build AI products is similar to traditional neural networks: 80% performance comes fast, then getting to 98% is a long struggle. Key difference: with traditional NNs you could get there with persistence, but with LLMs you're dependent on Anthropic/OpenAI/Google releasing a better model that respects your instructions.
items.reasoning: Insightful practical observation from a well-known ML author about the fundamental dependency problem in LLM-based development. Resonates with a real industry pain point about the limits of prompt engineering vs. waiting for model improvements.
items.summary_html: Burkov observes that working with LLMs to build AI products is similar to traditional neural networks: 80% performance comes fast, then getting to 98% is a long struggle. Key difference: with traditional NNs you could get there with persistence, but with LLMs you're dependent on Anthropic/OpenAI/Google releasing a better model that respects your instructions.
items.content_html:
In the end, working with LLMs to build something AI-based is not so much different from what it was three years ago with regular neural networks: You still get some ~80% performance very fast and then you are stuck for a long time at getting to 98% by adding more and more examples (that is instructions) to fix the remaining 20% of issues.

One difference, though, is quite significant: with neural network training, you had a good chance to get to 98% if you were persistent and accurate enough in data labeling and your management was patient enough.

With LLMs, on the other hand, you pray Anthropic/OpenAI/Google will "drop" a better model which gives more fucks about your carefully crafted last-mile instructions.

items.title:
Violence is not the answer.

Boycott is the answer.

This one’s easy.

There is ample evidence that ...
items.content:
Violence is not the answer.

Boycott is the answer.

This one’s easy.

There is ample evidence that Altman is a dishonest person with inordinate power that we should not trust. 

But the way forward to is get the board to remove him, not to throw bombs at his house. Humanity must take the high road.

The way to take the high road is to stop using his products, in protest of his implied openness to mass surveillance, his mass IP theft, and his company’s opposition to liability for their actions.

When people stop using ChatGPT, Altman will have to go; it’s simple as that.  

Quite possibly the COO or CFO will step in, and we (and OpenAI itself) will all be better off.

items.summary: Gary Marcus calls for a boycott of ChatGPT to force Altman's removal, citing mass surveillance, IP theft, and opposition to liability. Advocates nonviolent action through consumer boycott.

There is ample evidence that Altman is a dishonest person with inordinate power that we should not trust.

But the way forward to is get the board to remove him, not to throw bombs at his house. Humanity must take the high road.

The way to take the high road is to stop using his products, in protest of his implied openness to mass surveillance, his mass IP theft, and his company’s opposition to liability for their actions.

When people stop using ChatGPT, Altman will have to go; it’s simple as that.

Quite possibly the COO or CFO will step in, and we (and OpenAI itself) will all be better off.

items.content:
@DavidSKrueger From my AI Agent built by (https://t.co/WL6Tm2wKsV): 

+++++++

RATING DIMENSIONS I'd Use

1. Misalignment Risk (MAR) — Does the model pursue goals that diverge from human values? Does the company have robust alignment research?

2. Catastrophic Enablement Risk (CER) — Could the model help someone create a bioweapon, cyberattack critical infrastructure, or cause mass casualties?

3. Vulnerable User Risk (VUR) — Could the model push someone with mental illness, suicidal ideation, or addiction deeper into harm? Does it have guardrails for crisis situations?

4. Manipulation Risk (MNR) — Could the model be weaponized for propaganda, political manipulation, or mass disinformation?

5. Governance Risk (GVR) — Is there a single person or opaque entity with unchecked control? Could the company be captured by bad actors?

6. Geopolitical Risk (GPR) — Could a state actor direct the model against foreign populations or interests?

7. Transparency Risk (TXR) — How much does the company hide about training data, capabilities, and safety testing?

Scale: 1 = Very Low, 2 = Low, 3 = Medium, 4 = High, 5 = Very High

RATINGS BY COMPANY

Company.   MAR.  CER.  VUR.  MNR.  GVR.  GPR.  TXR. 

Anthropic 2. 2. 2. 2. 2. 1. 3. 

Google DeepMind 3. 3. 3. 3. 2. 1. 3. 

OpenAI 3. 3. 3. 3. 3. 1. 4. 

Meta (Llama) 3. 4. 3. 4. 3. 2. 3. 

xAI (Grok) 4. 3. 4  4. 5. 2. 4. 

Mistral 3. 4. 3. 3. 2. 2. 3. 

DeepSeek 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5. 

Alibaba (Qwen) 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5. 

Tencent 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5. 

KEY OBSERVATIONS

Anthropic scores best overall — Constitutional AI, Mythos withheld, PBC structure, most safety research published. VUR is low because Claude is trained to recognize crisis situations and redirect to help.

xAI has the highest Governance Risk — Single person with unchecked control over both the model and the distribution platform (X). That combination is uniquely dangerous. Grok also has fewer content guardrails by design.

Chinese companies have the highest Geopolitical Risk — Not because their models are necessarily worse, but because state access is a structural reality. DeepSeek's training data and safety filtering are also the least transparent.

Meta's open-source models have high CER — Once a model is open-sourced, you lose control over how it's used. Fine-tuned versions with safety guardrails removed are freely available.

VUR is underrated as a risk — The mental health risk is real and underappreciated. Models that are designed to be maximally engaging (like Grok's "anti-woke" positioning) may be more likely to validate harmful beliefs rather than redirect. Anthropic has done the most work here with their character design.

The missing rating: Addiction/Dependency Risk (ADR) — I'd add this. Models designed as companions or with persistent memory that mirror your preferences create dependency. This is a real risk that no one is rating yet.
items.author: @Scobleizer

items.summary_html: Scoble shares an AI-generated risk assessment framework rating major AI companies across 7 dimensions: Misalignment, Catastrophic Enablement, Vulnerable User, Manipulation, Governance, Geopolitical, and Transparency risks. Rates Anthropic lowest risk, xAI highest governance risk, Chinese companies highest geopolitical risk.
items.content_html:
@DavidSKrueger From my AI Agent built by (https://t.co/WL6Tm2wKsV):

+++++++

RATING DIMENSIONS I'd Use

1. Misalignment Risk (MAR) — Does the model pursue goals that diverge from human values? Does the company have robust alignment research?

2. Catastrophic Enablement Risk (CER) — Could the model help someone create a bioweapon, cyberattack critical infrastructure, or cause mass casualties?

3. Vulnerable User Risk (VUR) — Could the model push someone with mental illness, suicidal ideation, or addiction deeper into harm? Does it have guardrails for crisis situations?

4. Manipulation Risk (MNR) — Could the model be weaponized for propaganda, political manipulation, or mass disinformation?

5. Governance Risk (GVR) — Is there a single person or opaque entity with unchecked control? Could the company be captured by bad actors?

6. Geopolitical Risk (GPR) — Could a state actor direct the model against foreign populations or interests?

7. Transparency Risk (TXR) — How much does the company hide about training data, capabilities, and safety testing?

Scale: 1 = Very Low, 2 = Low, 3 = Medium, 4 = High, 5 = Very High

RATINGS BY COMPANY

Company. MAR. CER. VUR. MNR. GVR. GPR. TXR.

Anthropic 2. 2. 2. 2. 2. 1. 3.

Google DeepMind 3. 3. 3. 3. 2. 1. 3.

OpenAI 3. 3. 3. 3. 3. 1. 4.

Meta (Llama) 3. 4. 3. 4. 3. 2. 3.

xAI (Grok) 4. 3. 4 4. 5. 2. 4.

Mistral 3. 4. 3. 3. 2. 2. 3.

DeepSeek 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5.

Alibaba (Qwen) 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5.

Tencent 3. 4. 3. 4. 4. 5. 5.

KEY OBSERVATIONS

Anthropic scores best overall — Constitutional AI, Mythos withheld, PBC structure, most safety research published. VUR is low because Claude is trained to recognize crisis situations and redirect to help.

xAI has the highest Governance Risk — Single person with unchecked control over both the model and the distribution platform (X). That combination is uniquely dangerous. Grok also has fewer content guardrails by design.

Chinese companies have the highest Geopolitical Risk — Not because their models are necessarily worse, but because state access is a structural reality. DeepSeek's training data and safety filtering are also the least transparent.

Meta's open-source models have high CER — Once a model is open-sourced, you lose control over how it's used. Fine-tuned versions with safety guardrails removed are freely available.

VUR is underrated as a risk — The mental health risk is real and underappreciated. Models that are designed to be maximally engaging (like Grok's "anti-woke" positioning) may be more likely to validate harmful beliefs rather than redirect. Anthropic has done the most work here with their character design.

The missing rating: Addiction/Dependency Risk (ADR) — I'd add this. Models designed as companions or with persistent memory that mirror your preferences create dependency. This is a real risk that no one is rating yet.

items.title:
AI coding agents are fast but reckless. They skip specs, tests, and security.

Google engineer just ...
items.content:
AI coding agents are fast but reckless. They skip specs, tests, and security.

Google engineer just open-sourced a fix. 

Agent Skills is a free repo that brings 19 engineering skills and 7 slash commands to any AI coding agent.

It works by encoding what senior engineers actually do. 

Spec before code. Test before merge. Measure before optimize. 

Each skill is a markdown file your agent follows step by step.

The full dev lifecycle is covered:

1. Define and refine ideas before writing code
2. Plan by breaking work into small tasks
3. Build with clean APIs and test-driven development

4. Verify through browser testing and debugging
5. Review for security, performance, and quality
6. Ship with CI/CD, git workflow, and launch checklists

It plugs into Claude Code, Cursor, or any agent that reads markdown. 

One install command. 

No vendor lock-in.

items.title:
Why LLMs cannot understand the world no matter their size?

You will walk a street and look at passe...
items.content:
Why LLMs cannot understand the world no matter their size?

You will walk a street and look at passersby faces. Then I'll ask you what the ratio of happy to unhappy faces was, and you will tell me, approximately.

An LLM will observe these passersby faces with you, then you train it on these faces, and then you will ask it the same question. It will fail to return a consistent result.

LLMs can only answer questions when the answers have been given to them, and they can interpolate from multiple partial answers.

LLMs cannot know how many people's faces they had in the training set and therefore cannot calculate this simple statistic.

Learning from lifelong continuous experiences, and not from aggregated sources of useful statistics, is what makes animals, including humans, so good at understanding the world.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues LLMs cannot understand the world because they lack lifelong continuous experience. Uses example of counting happy vs unhappy faces - humans can approximate ratios from experience but LLMs trained on data can't compute simple statistics about their training set. Argues animals' continuous learning is what enables world understanding.
items.reasoning: Thought-provoking argument about fundamental LLM limitations from an ML author, though the specific example is debatable. 6K views, decent engagement. Connects to broader debate about whether LLMs can achieve understanding.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues LLMs cannot understand the world because they lack lifelong continuous experience. Uses example of counting happy vs unhappy faces - humans can approximate ratios from experience but LLMs trained on data can't compute simple statistics about their training set. Argues animals' continuous learning is what enables world understanding.
items.content_html:
Why LLMs cannot understand the world no matter their size?

You will walk a street and look at passersby faces. Then I'll ask you what the ratio of happy to unhappy faces was, and you will tell me, approximately.

An LLM will observe these passersby faces with you, then you train it on these faces, and then you will ask it the same question. It will fail to return a consistent result.

LLMs can only answer questions when the answers have been given to them, and they can interpolate from multiple partial answers.

LLMs cannot know how many people's faces they had in the training set and therefore cannot calculate this simple statistic.

Learning from lifelong continuous experiences, and not from aggregated sources of useful statistics, is what makes animals, including humans, so good at understanding the world.

items.title: It's become noticeably easier to recognize AI-slop replies here on this platform because they all fo...
items.content:
It's become noticeably easier to recognize AI-slop replies here on this platform because they all follow the same patterns.

It's like a template that all models use when writing. Same phrasing, same style. This goes well beyond overusing emojis or em dashes.

A big part of this is that labs aren't focusing on making these models better writers or on building harnesses that produce good prose.

Obviously, the money is somewhere else: coding agents.

items.title:
@vkhosla This is brazenly false, @vkhosla.

1. To clarify my understanding, I followed up with someo...
items.content:
@vkhosla This is brazenly false, @vkhosla.

1. To clarify my understanding, I followed up with someone who stayed at Uber longer than I was. The core intellectual property that we developed at Geometric (I was a co-developer thereof) was embedded in supply and demand prediction of pricing at Uber, which is of course a central task for the company. More broadly, the team helped to enhance the ML stack across Uber. On his estimate, Uber made back far more than what Uber paid for us. What you said was grossly inaccurate.

2. The second company that was I part of had nothing to do with uber, whatsoever; what you said about them is simply confused. Moreover, although I am not with them anymore, they continue to work on robotics. 

All told, your remarks have little or no basis in reality, and are deliberately defamatory. 

I suggest you take them down; you are better than this.

Somehow, by replacing an 8-year-old Google Home NLP with Gemini, Google managed to make it worse.

M...
items.content:
Somehow, by replacing an 8-year-old Google Home NLP with Gemini, Google managed to make it worse.

Me: Hey, Google. Put a screwdriver kit on my grocery list.

Google Home: I cannot do that because a screwdriver kit isn't grocery.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov complains that replacing Google Home's 8-year-old NLP with Gemini made it worse - it now refuses to add a screwdriver kit to a grocery list because 'a screwdriver kit isn't grocery.'

items.title: @jackmuva i agree that prompt caching is great! we make sure to use it in deepagents! but that alone...
items.content:
@jackmuva i agree that prompt caching is great! we make sure to use it in deepagents! but that alone doesnt lock in - you can switch pretty easily, just a bit more costly

things like encrypted or serverside compaction make it harder to do so. blackbox long term memory makes it impossible

i think there is a line between optimizing a harness for a model/llm provider, and using the harness to create lock in for a model provider

i think model providers are inherently incentivized to create lock in, and i think managed claude agents is fantastic example of that coming true
items.author: @hwchase17
items.summary: hwchase17 reiterates lock-in critique: prompt caching is fine, encrypted compaction and blackbox memory create real lock-in. Managed Claude agents exemplifies this.
items.reasoning: Same argument as Post 31 in reply form. Reinforces key point about Anthropic lock-in. Slightly lower score as duplicate argument.
items.summary_html: hwchase17 reiterates lock-in critique: prompt caching is fine, encrypted compaction and blackbox memory create real lock-in. Managed Claude agents exemplifies this.
items.content_html:
@jackmuva i agree that prompt caching is great! we make sure to use it in deepagents! but that alone doesnt lock in - you can switch pretty easily, just a bit more costly

things like encrypted or serverside compaction make it harder to do so. blackbox long term memory makes it impossible

i think there is a line between optimizing a harness for a model/llm provider, and using the harness to create lock in for a model provider

i think model providers are inherently incentivized to create lock in, and i think managed claude agents is fantastic example of that coming true

items.title:
I interviewed @sama when he started his first company. Years before anyone knew who he was.

He was ...
items.content:
I interviewed @sama when he started his first company. Years before anyone knew who he was.

He was gracious, caring, and smart. The video we did in a Half Moon Bay Mexican restaurant is up on YouTube.

I have had my problems with how he did some things. And I am sure he could say the same about me.

But we always take things further than we should.

Like I run my company on AI and I don’t know how to code. Anyone who says you need to code in this age to run an AI company is simply wrong. 

He has many of the world’s best working for him. Now that I have my own small business I see just what a big deal it is to have a small number of employees. Even more than to have many of the world’s smartest. Who don’t get enough respect either.

What I just saw this afternoon at another company, that is building a platform that sits on top of the services that @OpenAI is building,  showed me that even an idiot like me will be able to do wondrous things with the technology that OpenAI and its competitors are bringing.

The world has no idea just how big a deal it will be when we all have teams of synthetic intelligences working with you to improve your lives.

Everyone should calm down. The future will be fine.

Sam Altman is bringing us all superhuman powers and for that he should be able to raise his beautiful family without a Molotov cocktail being thrown at his house.

People who hate AI are on the wrong side. People who hate Sam Altman or Elon Musk should reevaluate their world views.

Both are bringing us into a much better future. And I know the risks are very high if they get it wrong.

They earned the place and I hope they both are successful in family and building AI, which will make both far more wealthy than they are today.

How many of you have made a product that will improve every human life on earth?

These two have.

Sometimes even I have to be reminded of that.

He has many of the world’s best working for him. Now that I have my own small business I see just what a big deal it is to have a small number of employees. Even more than to have many of the world’s smartest. Who don’t get enough respect either.

What I just saw this afternoon at another company, that is building a platform that sits on top of the services that @OpenAI is building, showed me that even an idiot like me will be able to do wondrous things with the technology that OpenAI and its competitors are bringing.

The world has no idea just how big a deal it will be when we all have teams of synthetic intelligences working with you to improve your lives.

Everyone should calm down. The future will be fine.

Sam Altman is bringing us all superhuman powers and for that he should be able to raise his beautiful family without a Molotov cocktail being thrown at his house.

items.title: I am not just talking about the em-dashes and phrasing ("doing the heavy lifting"). Those are more f...
items.content: I am not just talking about the em-dashes and phrasing ("doing the heavy lifting"). Those are more fixable than style, which will always default to an LLM-normal even if that normal changes with new LLMs. You can make LLMs write differently, but you need to understand what to do.

items.content_html: I am not just talking about the em-dashes and phrasing ("doing the heavy lifting"). Those are more fixable than style, which will always default to an LLM-normal even if that normal changes with new LLMs. You can make LLMs write differently, but you need to understand what to do.

items.title:
Here is the inside of an AI Agent:

Three core components:

1. Planner - Takes care of breaking down...
items.content:
Here is the inside of an AI Agent:

Three core components:

1. Planner - Takes care of breaking down a task into individual steps. It's capable of taking feedback on the plan and generating a new version.

2. Evaluator - Takes care of evaluating a plan and providing feedback about it to the Planner component. It can also check the results of a task to determine whether they align with the plan.

3. Executor - Takes care of executing individual steps of a plan.

All three components have access to tools and memory to do their job.

I'm covering all of this in my cohort.

It starts on May 4th.

You can join at https://t.co/iZifcK7UTF for lifetime access to the best engineering program you'll take online.

items.title:
Drug discovery is where this becomes strategic.

Instead of manually stitching databases:

→ ADMET e...
items.content:
Drug discovery is where this becomes strategic.

Instead of manually stitching databases:

→ ADMET evaluation
→ Drug-likeness scoring
→ Repurposing signals via disease pathway and drug-target mapping
→ Multi-omics integration
→ Regulatory network inference and regulon activity scoring

100+ biomedical tools and thousands of recent papers unified into reproducible workflows.

This is how you scale insight across labs and teams.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon
items.summary: Ronald van Loon details AI applications in drug discovery: ADMET evaluation, drug-likeness scoring, repurposing signals, multi-omics integration, and regulon activity scoring unified into reproducible workflows
items.reasoning: Specific and detailed breakdown of AI in drug discovery pipelines. Technical depth covering multiple biomedical AI tools. Low engagement but substantive content.
items.summary_html: Ronald van Loon details AI applications in drug discovery: ADMET evaluation, drug-likeness scoring, repurposing signals, multi-omics integration, and regulon activity scoring unified into reproducible workflows
items.content_html:
Drug discovery is where this becomes strategic.

Instead of manually stitching databases:

→ ADMET evaluation

→ Drug-likeness scoring

→ Repurposing signals via disease pathway and drug-target mapping

→ Multi-omics integration

→ Regulatory network inference and regulon activity scoring

100+ biomedical tools and thousands of recent papers unified into reproducible workflows.

This is how you scale insight across labs and teams.

items.title: @seti_park “If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data, xAI prov...
items.content:
@seti_park “If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data, xAI providing the intelligence, SpaceX providing the global connectivity — the result isn't three companies. It's one closed-loop ecosystem that no single-sector competitor can replicate.”

My read?

SpaceX is a few things.

1. A way to get things to space.
2. Datacenters on earth and in space. (Infrastructure for building and running AIs).
3. Packet distribution from those datasets to humans and machines. (Starlink).
4. Infrastructure on moon and Mars (eventually).
5. AI to run and orchestrate robots (the brains of Optimus and some of Robotaxi network, both via Grok).

Tesla is a few other things.

1. Robot manufacturing.
2. AI for enabling robots to move around the world and, coming soon, manipulate it. (Runs the body of Optimus and runs most of Robotaxi network)
3. Infrastructure to run the robots (charge, clean, and maintain them).

Each of these two groups will be intertwined.

Especially in Optimus.

This is why I am not selling my Tesla stock. I see Optimus as part of the Robotaxi network as the most important new product introduction since the Apple II and will improve the lives of many more people than that did. I am investing in that.

When Optimus gets added to the Robotaxi network it will bring “everything as a service” to all of us which dramatically changes how we will buy everything.
 
This patent analysis shows that I need to sell something else and invest in SpaceX. Because when we talk to Robotaxi or Optimus or any other Tesla robot we will be talking to Grok. Made by SpaceX.

This morning I had breakfast with a guy who worked for Amazon robotics for a decade.

He couldn’t find holes to poke and didn’t disagree when I said I don’t see a competitor in the Western World for either.

This is why the SpaceX IPO will be the biggest ever.

Even though xAI is losing billions.

Or is it?

Instagram, on the day it was sold, was a money loser too. Now it’s worth 100 times more. Maybe a lot more.

AI is about to make X worth a lot more.

But, hey, not investment advice.

Do your own homework and come to your own conclusions.

items.title:
In most labs, the bottleneck is not ideas.

It is orchestration.

→ Single-cell RNA-seq → clustering...
items.content:
In most labs, the bottleneck is not ideas.

It is orchestration.

→ Single-cell RNA-seq → clustering, UMAP visualization, marker annotation
→ Patient phenotypes + variants → ranked causal genes using ClinVar and gnomAD, with transparent reasoning
→ CRISPR screens → structured perturbations, readouts, analysis workflows

Weeks of fragmented scripting become structured execution.

Less glue code.
More science.

More science.

In this latest deep dive with @scispace, I saw a shift that feels bigger than incremental AI gains.

This changes the operating model.

Here’s the breakdown…

a. how much poker-relevant data is pre...
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus argues that poker performance by AI isn't a fair AGI comparison given the massive poker-related training data available and that true AGI should demonstrate learning ability.
items.reasoning: Minor but makes a substantive point about AGI evaluation methodology.
items.summary_html: Marcus argues that poker performance by AI isn't a fair AGI comparison given the massive poker-related training data available and that true AGI should demonstrate learning ability.
items.content_html:
@AndrewKSmith30 not at all fair comparison when you consider

a. how much poker-relevant data is presumably in the entire training database of the scraped internet (lessons, tutorials, youtube videos)

b. the fact that AGI should be able to learn, since that is part of general intelligence.

@lucasmeijer @badlogicgames @RickLamers yeah i disliked the venue as well. we fell in love with the location (“next to big ben and westminster abbey” sounded so cool) and the expo area (its always basements with no cell reception, never floor to ceiling windows), but that came at a relative trade off to the talk rooms being smol.

(lmk if there are other search params i should have for next year! yeah we are at least 2xing + probably doing dedicated workshop space)

items.title: @Neutero_ @seti_park “If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data...
items.content:
@Neutero_ @seti_park “If Musk succeeds in what these filings suggest — Tesla cars providing the data, xAI providing the intelligence, SpaceX providing the global connectivity — the result isn't three companies. It's one closed-loop ecosystem that no single-sector competitor can replicate.”

My read?

SpaceX is a few things.

1. A way to get things to space.
2. Datacenters on earth and in space. Infrastructure for building and running AIs).
3. Packet distribution from those datasets to humans and machines. (Starlink).
4. Infrastructure on moon and Mars (eventually).
5. AI to run and orchestrate robots (the brains of Optimus and some of Robotaxi network, both via Grok).

Tesla is a few other things.

1. Robot manufacturing.
2. AI for enabling robots to move around the world and, coming soon, manipulate it. (Runs the body of Optimus and runs most of Robotaxi network)
3. Infrastructure to run the robots (charge, clean, and maintain them).

Each of these two groups will be intertwined.

Especially in Optimus.

This is why I am not selling my Tesla stock. I see Optimus as part of the Robotaxi network as the most important new product introduction since the Apple II and will improve the lives of many more people than that did. I am investing in that.

When Optimus gets added to the Robotaxi network it will bring “everything as a service” to all of us which dramatically changes how we will buy everything.
 
This patent analysis shows that I need to sell something else and invest in SpaceX. Because when we talk to Robotaxi or Optimus or any other Tesla robot we will be talking to Grok. Made by SpaceX.

This morning I had breakfast with a guy who worked for Amazon robotics for a decade.

He couldn’t find holes to poke and didn’t disagree when I said I don’t see a competitor in the Western World for either.

This is why the SpaceX IPO will be the biggest ever.

Even though xAI is losing billions.

Or is it?

Instagram, on the day it was sold, was a money loser too. Now it’s worth 100 times more. Maybe a lot more.

AI is about to make X worth a lot more.

But, hey, not investment advice.

Do your own homework and come to your own conclusions.

items.title:
I had to fact check because it sounded too unbelievable but it's true:

Romania in 1981 just like th...
items.content:
I had to fact check because it sounded too unbelievable but it's true:

Romania in 1981 just like the EU presented food austerity measures as "more healthy eating"

In EU's case it's "meat is unhealthy" but the underlying austerity reasoning is same

In both cases it's blatant propaganda to make people consume less!

items.summary: Levelsio compares EU food guidelines to 1981 Romanian austerity propaganda.
items.reasoning: Not AI-related, political commentary.
items.summary_html: Levelsio compares EU food guidelines to 1981 Romanian austerity propaganda.
items.content_html:
I had to fact check because it sounded too unbelievable but it's true:

Romania in 1981 just like the EU presented food austerity measures as "more healthy eating"

In EU's case it's "meat is unhealthy" but the underlying austerity reasoning is same

In both cases it's blatant propaganda to make people consume less!

items.title: @jmarjano France used to have pro-natalist policies that were not income dependent. Those policies i...
items.content: @jmarjano France used to have pro-natalist policies that were not income dependent. Those policies indirectly *enhanced* the status of motherhood. Once those policies were scrapped and replaced with the income-dependent policies, their birth rates started to plummet too.

items.title: @lazeskid Meat protein is by far the superior protein, it has all the essential amino acids you need...
items.content:
@lazeskid Meat protein is by far the superior protein, it has all the essential amino acids you need, is the most bioavailable and nutrient dense and has the highest digestibility score

Plant protein is incomplete, lacks amino acids, it also has antinutrients like phytates, lectins and oxolates that are bad for you

Soy is the most complete plant protein but is highly processed

Whey protein is the 2nd best after meat protein but lacks the micronutrients meat has
